title: Get started
text:
Prerequisites: R ≥ 4.2, cmake, and Rust stable (via rustup).
The standard build uses FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1, which skips the Enzyme toolchain and falls back to finite-difference gradients. Estimates are identical; compile time is ~2 minutes. Enzyme (exact autodiff) is optional and documented below.


brew install cmake
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
RStudio on macOS does not inherit the Homebrew PATH. Add cmake explicitly before installing:
# Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3):
Sys.setenv(PATH = paste("/opt/homebrew/opt/cmake/bin", Sys.getenv("PATH"), sep = ":"))
# Intel Mac — use this line instead:
# Sys.setenv(PATH = paste("/usr/local/opt/cmake/bin", Sys.getenv("PATH"), sep = ":"))

Sys.setenv(FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1")
devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")



Install Rtools44 and Rust via rustup. cmake ships with Rtools44.
Sys.setenv(FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1")
devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")



sudo apt-get install cmake   # Debian / Ubuntu
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
Sys.setenv(FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1")
devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")

For full autodiff accuracy, a one-time Enzyme toolchain build is required. Pick your platform:

Linux — fully supported, ~30 min plugin build
macOS — supported with caveats
Windows — not natively supported; use WSL2, Docker, or finite-difference fallback
Docker — Enzyme pre-installed, recommended for Windows autodiff
Multi-user Linux server

title: Get started
text:
Prerequisites: R ≥ 4.2, cmake, and Rust stable (via rustup).
The standard build uses FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1, which skips the Enzyme toolchain and falls back to finite-difference gradients. Estimates are identical; compile time is ~2 minutes. Enzyme (exact autodiff) is optional and documented below.


brew install cmake
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
RStudio on macOS does not inherit the Homebrew PATH. Add cmake explicitly before installing:
# Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3):
Sys.setenv(PATH = paste("/opt/homebrew/opt/cmake/bin", Sys.getenv("PATH"), sep = ":"))
# Intel Mac — use this line instead:
# Sys.setenv(PATH = paste("/usr/local/opt/cmake/bin", Sys.getenv("PATH"), sep = ":"))

Sys.setenv(FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1")
devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")



Install Rtools44 and Rust via rustup. cmake ships with Rtools44.
Sys.setenv(FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1")
devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")



sudo apt-get install cmake   # Debian / Ubuntu
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
Sys.setenv(FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1")
devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")

For full autodiff accuracy, a one-time Enzyme toolchain build is required. Pick your platform:

Linux — fully supported, ~30 min plugin build
macOS — supported with caveats
Windows — not natively supported; use WSL2, Docker, or finite-difference fallback
Docker — Enzyme pre-installed, recommended for Windows autodiff
Multi-user Linux server

title: Get started
text:
Linux
Tested on Ubuntu 22.04. Other distributions (Debian, Fedora, Arch) work with adjustments to the package manager commands.

1. Install rustup + nightly






Warning



Do not use snap’s rustup — its filesystem confinement breaks on non-standard home directories common on servers.


# Remove snap rustup if present:
sudo snap remove rustup

# Official installer
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh -s -- -y
source "$HOME/.cargo/env"

rustup toolchain install nightly


2. Install system dependencies + matching LLVM
First check which LLVM major version nightly needs:
rustc +nightly --version --verbose | grep LLVM
# e.g. "LLVM version: 22.1.2" — use major = 22
Use that <MAJOR> below:
sudo apt install -y cmake ninja-build clang libssl-dev pkg-config \
                    python3 build-essential curl git libzstd-dev

# Install matching LLVM from apt.llvm.org (Ubuntu's defaults often lag):
wget https://apt.llvm.org/llvm.sh
chmod +x llvm.sh
sudo ./llvm.sh <MAJOR>

sudo chmod 644 /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/apt.llvm.org.asc
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y llvm-<MAJOR>-dev clang-<MAJOR>
For Fedora/RHEL: sudo dnf install llvm<MAJOR>-devel clang<MAJOR>.


3. Build the Enzyme plugin (~15–30 min)
git clone https://github.com/EnzymeAD/Enzyme /tmp/enzyme-build
cd /tmp/enzyme-build/enzyme
mkdir build && cd build

cmake -G Ninja .. \
  -DLLVM_DIR=/usr/lib/llvm-<MAJOR>/lib/cmake/llvm \
  -DENZYME_CLANG=OFF \
  -DENZYME_FLANG=OFF
ninja


4. Install the plugin into nightly’s sysroot
SYSROOT=$(rustc +nightly --print sysroot)
TARGET=x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu   # or aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu on ARM

cp /tmp/enzyme-build/enzyme/build/Enzyme/LLVMEnzyme-<MAJOR>.so \
   $SYSROOT/lib/rustlib/$TARGET/lib/libEnzyme-<MAJOR>.so
Note the filename rewrite: LLVMEnzyme-<N>.so → libEnzyme-<N>.so (the lib prefix is required).


5. Register the toolchain as enzyme
rustup toolchain link enzyme "$(rustc +nightly --print sysroot)"


6. Verify
rustc +enzyme -Z autodiff=Enable - </dev/null 2>&1 | head
Expected output: error[E0601]: 'main' function not found. That is the success signal. If you see autodiff backend not found in the sysroot, the .so is missing or placed in the wrong directory (step 4).

title: Get started
text:
macOS
Supported on both Intel and Apple Silicon.

1. Install Xcode Command Line Tools
xcode-select --install


2. Install rustup + nightly
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh -s -- -y
source "$HOME/.cargo/env"
rustup toolchain install nightly


3. Install matching LLVM via Homebrew
rustc +nightly --version --verbose | grep LLVM
# note the major version, e.g. 22
brew install llvm@<MAJOR> cmake ninja
Homebrew ships a limited set of LLVM versions. If your version is unavailable, install a slightly older dated nightly that matches what Homebrew has:
rustup toolchain install nightly-YYYY-MM-DD


4. Build the Enzyme plugin
git clone https://github.com/EnzymeAD/Enzyme /tmp/enzyme-build
cd /tmp/enzyme-build/enzyme
mkdir build && cd build

# Adjust prefix: /opt/homebrew (Apple Silicon) or /usr/local (Intel)
cmake -G Ninja .. \
  -DLLVM_DIR=$(brew --prefix)/opt/llvm@<MAJOR>/lib/cmake/llvm \
  -DENZYME_CLANG=OFF \
  -DENZYME_FLANG=OFF
ninja


5. Install the plugin
SYSROOT=$(rustc +nightly --print sysroot)

# Apple Silicon:
TARGET=aarch64-apple-darwin
# Intel: TARGET=x86_64-apple-darwin

# macOS uses .dylib, not .so
cp /tmp/enzyme-build/enzyme/build/Enzyme/LLVMEnzyme-<MAJOR>.dylib \
   $SYSROOT/lib/rustlib/$TARGET/lib/libEnzyme-<MAJOR>.dylib


6. Register and verify
rustup toolchain link enzyme "$(rustc +nightly --print sysroot)"
rustc +enzyme -Z autodiff=Enable - </dev/null 2>&1 | head
# Expect: error[E0601]: `main` function not found






Note



macOS caveats

Code-signing errors loading the .dylib: run sudo codesign --force --sign - <path-to-libEnzyme.dylib> — rare on developer machines.
Apple Silicon nightly lag: ARM nightlies occasionally trail x86_64 by a day or two; if LLVM versions mismatch after rustup update, pin a specific dated nightly.

title: Get started
text:
Windows
Windows is not currently supported for the full Enzyme autodiff backend. Two practical options are available:

Option A — WSL2 (recommended for full autodiff)
Install Ubuntu under WSL2 and follow the Linux instructions. Performance is near-native for CPU-bound workloads like model fitting.
# From PowerShell (admin):
wsl --install -d Ubuntu-22.04
Then open a WSL terminal and follow the Linux steps above.


Option B — Native Windows (without Enzyme, finite differences only)
Gradients fall back to finite differences — slower and less accurate than autodiff, but the R package is otherwise fully functional.
Prerequisites:

R and Rtools44 (ships MinGW gcc compatible with R’s linker)
rustup with the GNU-ABI toolchain:

rustup toolchain install stable-x86_64-pc-windows-gnu
You do not need to make it the default — the package build pins this toolchain automatically on Windows.
Then install in R:
devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")


Option C — Docker (full autodiff on Windows)
The Docker image bundles the Enzyme toolchain, ferx CLI, the R package, and RStudio Server. See Docker below.






Note



Full native Windows support is blocked on upstream Enzyme integration in rustup (rust-lang/rust#124509). Once that lands, the plugin distribution problem disappears and Windows support becomes straightforward.

title: Get started
text:
Docker
The Docker image ships with the full Enzyme toolchain pre-built — no local Rust or Enzyme setup required. Recommended for Windows users who need autodiff, or any environment where you want a reproducible, isolated setup.
# Build the image (first build ~45–60 min; subsequent builds use the cache)
docker build -t ferx:latest https://github.com/FeRx-NLME/ferx-r.git#main

# Run RStudio Server
docker run --rm -p 8787:8787 -e PASSWORD=ferx ferx:latest
Open http://localhost:8787 — user: rstudio, password: ferx.
To mount a local data directory:
docker run --rm -p 8787:8787 -e PASSWORD=ferx \
  -v /path/to/your/data:/home/rstudio/data \
  ferx:latest

title: Get started
text:
Multi-user Linux server
For shared servers, build the toolchain once and stage it in /opt/rust-nightly so all users share a single installation.
# As root (after completing the Linux steps above):
sudo mkdir -p /opt/rust-nightly
sudo cp -a ~/.rustup/toolchains/nightly-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/. /opt/rust-nightly/
sudo chown -R root:root /opt/rust-nightly
sudo chmod -R a+rX /opt/rust-nightly
sudo chmod a+rx /opt/rust-nightly/bin/*
Each user links the shared toolchain into their own rustup:
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh -s -- -y --default-toolchain none
rustup toolchain link enzyme /opt/rust-nightly
Add to each user’s ~/.Renviron:
PATH=/opt/rust-nightly/bin:${HOME}/.cargo/bin:${PATH}
RUSTUP_TOOLCHAIN=enzyme
See INSTALL-SYSADMIN.md for quarterly refresh procedures and troubleshooting shared installs.

title: Get started
text:
After toolchain setup
Once your Enzyme toolchain is in place, install the R package:
devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
Then run the warfarin example:
library(ferx)
ex  <- ferx_example("warfarin")
fit <- ferx_fit(ex$model, ex$data)
print(fit)
summary(fit)
To see all bundled examples:
ferx_example()   # returns a character vector of all available example names
?ferx_example    # full list with descriptions in the help page

title: Get started
text:
Troubleshooting
"autodiff backend not found in the sysroot" The .so/.dylib is missing or in the wrong location. It must be in <sysroot>/lib/rustlib/<target>/lib/libEnzyme-<N>.so — not just lib/.
"error: the option 'Z' is only accepted on the nightly compiler" Your shell (or R) is finding a stable rustc. Check rustc --version, your PATH, and ~/.Renviron.
"custom toolchain 'enzyme' is not installed" Run: rustup toolchain link enzyme $(rustc +nightly --print sysroot)
"incorrect value 'X' for unstable option 'autodiff'" Valid values change between nightlies. Verify with:
rustc +enzyme -Z autodiff=Enable - </dev/null 2>&1 | head
For more troubleshooting detail see the ferx-core installation docs.

title: Get started
text:
Next steps

Book — comprehensive narrative guide with runnable examples
Model workflow — full fitting workflow
Model DSL — write your own .ferx model files
Reference — function documentation

title: Model workflow: inspect, edit, fit
text:
This article covers two complementary workflows for running a ferx model:
Both produce the same ferx_fit result.

title: Model workflow: inspect, edit, fit
text:
Path-based workflow

Step 1 — Get a model file
ferx_example() returns paths to the bundled example models:
ex <- ferx_example("warfarin")
ex$model  # path to warfarin.ferx
ex$data   # path to warfarin.csv


Step 2 — Inspect before fitting
ferx_model_inspect() parses the model and prints a concise summary without running the optimizer:
ferx_model_inspect(ex$model)



Line
Meaning




Structural
PK model type and theta names


IIV
Omega parameter names


IOV
Kappa parameter names, or none


Residual
Error model type



Returns the structure as a named list (invisibly):
s <- ferx_model_inspect(ex$model)
s$theta_names   # population parameter names
s$model_type    # short label for the PK model
s$iiv           # IIV parameter names
s$residual      # error model type


Step 3 — Fit
fit <- ferx_fit(ex$model, ex$data)
fit


Step 4 — Re-inspect from the fitted result
After fitting you can call ferx_model_inspect() directly on the ferx_fit object — no need to supply the model path again:
ferx_model_inspect(fit)

title: Model workflow: inspect, edit, fit
text:
Pipe-based workflow
ferx_model() bundles model and data paths into a single object. Entry point: ferx_model(data, model) — data first, so it flows naturally into a pipeline:
ex$data |> ferx_model(ex$model) |> ferx_fit() |> summary()

Minimal pipe
ferx_model(ex$data, ex$model) |>
  ferx_fit(method = "focei", covariance = TRUE) |>
  summary()


Peek at a section mid-pipe
ferx_get_section() prints a named section and passes the object through:
ferx_model(ex$data, ex$model) |>
  ferx_get_section("parameters") |>
  ferx_fit(method = "focei")


Override fit options before fitting
ferx_set_section() rewrites a section on disk and returns the object unchanged. Copy the file first if you do not want to modify the original:
model_copy <- file.path(tempdir(), "warfarin.ferx")
file.copy(ex$model, model_copy)

fit <- ferx_model(ex$data, model_copy) |>
  ferx_set_section("fit_options", c(
    "  method     = focei",
    "  maxiter    = 500",
    "  covariance = true"
  )) |>
  ferx_fit()


Quick convergence check
ferx_check_init() runs only 5 iterations (20 for SAEM) and returns a trace and summary table. Use it to confirm the OFV is dropping before a long run:
chk <- ferx_check_init(ex$model, ex$data, method = "focei")
chk$summary      # n_iter, ofv_start, ofv_end, ofv_drop, converged
ferx_plot_trace(chk$fit)   # visual check of the first few iterations


Multi-stage method chain
fit <- ferx_model(ex$data, ex$model) |>
  ferx_fit(method = c("saem", "focei"), covariance = TRUE)
SAEM finds the basin; FOCEI polishes to the MLE and produces a valid covariance matrix. Recommended for complex models.

title: Model workflow: inspect, edit, fit
text:
Simulation and prediction
# Simulate 100 replicates using individual parameters from the fit
sim <- ferx_simulate(ex$model, ex$data, n_sim = 100, seed = 42, fit = fit)

# Population predictions using fitted theta (eta = 0)
pred <- ferx_predict(ex$model, ex$data, fit = fit)
See vignette("learn/simulation") or Simulation for a full worked example.

title: Model workflow: inspect, edit, fit
text:
Post-fit diagnostics
All diagnostic outputs are fields on the ferx_fit object:
head(fit$sdtab)                # PRED, IPRED, CWRES, IWRES per observation
head(fit$ebe_etas)             # empirical Bayes ETAs per subject
head(fit$individual_estimates) # individual PK parameters (CL, V, KA, ...)
fit$eta_normality              # Shapiro-Wilk normality test per ETA
ferx_estimates() collects all estimated parameters into a tidy data frame with SEs, %RSE, and 95% CIs:
ferx_estimates(fit)
#   param           transform  estimate     se  rse_pct  lower_95  upper_95
#   TVCL            identity     0.134  0.0108     8.1     0.113     0.155
#   TVV             identity     7.94   0.420      5.3     7.12      8.76
#   TVKA            identity     1.07   0.160     14.9     0.756     1.38
#   ETA_CL          variance     0.042  0.012     28.6    0.019     0.066
#   ETA_V           variance     0.020  0.009     44.9    0.002     0.037
#   ETA_KA          variance     0.157  0.048     30.6    0.063     0.251
#   PROP_ERR        proportional 0.011  0.001      9.1    0.009     0.013
ferx_cor_matrix() converts the parameter covariance matrix to correlations. A value near ±1 signals a structural identifiability problem:
ferx_cor_matrix(fit)
Goodness-of-fit plots from fit$sdtab with ggplot2:
library(ggplot2)
sdtab <- fit$sdtab

ggplot(sdtab, aes(PRED, DV)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "DV vs PRED")

ggplot(sdtab, aes(IPRED, DV)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "DV vs IPRED")

ggplot(sdtab, aes(TIME, CWRES)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 0, 2),
             linetype = c("dashed", "solid", "dashed"), colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "CWRES vs TIME")

ggplot(sdtab, aes(PRED, CWRES)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 0, 2),
             linetype = c("dashed", "solid", "dashed"), colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "CWRES vs PRED")
ETA-covariate correlations — identifies covariates worth testing in a formal covariate search. The warfarin dataset has no covariate columns; use two_cpt_oral_cov (which has WT and CRCL) to exercise ferx_eta_cov():
ex_cov   <- ferx_example("two_cpt_oral_cov")
fit_cov  <- ferx_fit(ex_cov$model, ex_cov$data)
data_cov <- read.csv(ex_cov$data)
ferx_eta_cov(fit_cov, data_cov)
#   eta      covariate    r   p_val  flag
#   ETA_CL   WT        0.38  0.008   [!]
#   ETA_CL   CRCL      0.41  0.003   [!]
#   ETA_V1   WT        0.29  0.047
# [!] flags |r| >= 0.3
See Diagnostics for the full diagnostics reference.

title: Model workflow: inspect, edit, fit
text:
Quick reference

Path-based
ex <- ferx_example("warfarin")
ferx_model_inspect(ex$model)          # verify structure before fitting
fit <- ferx_fit(ex$model, ex$data)
ferx_model_inspect(fit)               # re-inspect post-fit


Pipe-based
ex <- ferx_example("warfarin")

ferx_model(ex$data, ex$model) |>
  ferx_get_section("parameters") |>
  ferx_fit(method = "focei", covariance = TRUE) |>
  summary()

title: Editing ferx model files programmatically
text: ferx model files (.ferx) are plain text, so you can create and modify them entirely from R without opening an editor. This article covers the functions that support that workflow.

title: Editing ferx model files programmatically
text:
Starting from scratch
ferx_model_new() writes a skeleton .ferx file for one of five built-in templates: "1cpt_oral" (default), "1cpt_iv", "2cpt_oral", "2cpt_iv", and "ode".
Pass print = TRUE to preview a template in the console without writing any file:
ferx_model_new(print = TRUE)
ferx_model_new(template = "2cpt_iv", print = TRUE)
To write a file, supply a path. Pass edit = FALSE to skip opening your editor:
ferx_model_new("my_model.ferx", edit = FALSE)

title: Editing ferx model files programmatically
text:
Inspecting a section
ferx_model_section() extracts and prints the body of a named section:
ex <- ferx_example("warfarin")
ferx_model_section(ex$model, "parameters")
The return value is the character vector of lines (invisibly):
params <- ferx_model_section(ex$model, "parameters")
params

title: Editing ferx model files programmatically
text:
Scripted read-modify-write
The extract → modify → write-back pattern changes a model programmatically without an editor:
# Work in a fresh temp file
model_path <- tempfile(fileext = ".ferx")
file.copy(ex$model, model_path)

# Read the section
lines <- ferx_model_section(model_path, "parameters")

# Modify: raise the TVCL initial value from 0.134 to 0.5
lines <- sub("TVCL\\([^,]+,", "TVCL(0.5,", lines)

# Write back
ferx_model_set_section(model_path, "parameters", lines)

# Verify
ferx_model_section(model_path, "parameters")
ferx_model_set_section() replaces only the targeted section; all other sections are left untouched.

title: Editing ferx model files programmatically
text:
Console-only workflow: create, tweak, fit
From nothing to an estimated model without opening a text editor:
model_path <- file.path(tempdir(), "run1.ferx")

# 1. Write a skeleton
ferx_model_new(model_path, template = "1cpt_oral", edit = FALSE)

# 2. Switch method to FOCEI
ferx_model_set_section(model_path, "fit_options", c(
  "  method     = focei",
  "  maxiter    = 300",
  "  covariance = true"
))

# 3. Fit
ex  <- ferx_example("warfarin")
fit <- ferx_fit(model_path, ex$data)
print(fit)

title: Editing ferx model files programmatically
section: Opening a model in an editor
text:
Opening a model in an editor
ferx_model_edit() copies a bundled example to a destination directory and opens it in your editor:
# Copy the bundled warfarin model to tempdir() and open it
my_model <- ferx_model_edit(ex$model, dest = tempdir())

# After editing, save the result under a new name
ferx_model_edit("run1.ferx", save_as = "run2.ferx")
The dest argument controls where the copy lands. If the source file is already outside the installed package directory, ferx_model_edit() edits it in place unless dest is set explicitly.

title: Editing ferx model files programmatically
text:
Validating a model file
ferx_model_validate() checks that all required sections are present and reports the result. Use it after creating or editing a model to catch missing sections before running the optimizer:
ferx_model_validate(ex$model)
#> [ok] All required sections present: parameters, individual_parameters,
#>      structural_model, error_model
A model missing required sections produces a clear list:
bad <- tempfile(fileext = ".ferx")
writeLines(c(
  "[parameters]",
  "  theta TVCL(1.0, 0.001, 100.0)",
  "[structural_model]",
  "  pk one_cpt_oral(cl=CL, v=V, ka=KA)"
), bad)
ferx_model_validate(bad)
#> [missing] individual_parameters
#> [missing] error_model

title: Editing ferx model files programmatically
text:
Overwrite guards
By default ferx_model_new() refuses to overwrite an existing file:
model_path <- tempfile(fileext = ".ferx")
ferx_model_new(model_path, edit = FALSE)            # creates the file

tryCatch(
  ferx_model_new(model_path, edit = FALSE),          # would overwrite — error
  error = function(e) message(conditionMessage(e))
)
#> Error: file already exists. Use overwrite = TRUE to replace it.

# Opt in explicitly:
ferx_model_new(model_path, template = "1cpt_iv", overwrite = TRUE, edit = FALSE)

title: Editing ferx model files programmatically
text:
Function reference







Function
Purpose




ferx_model_new()
Write a skeleton .ferx file from a template


ferx_model_section()
Extract the body of a named section


ferx_model_set_section()
Replace the body of a named section


ferx_get_section()
Print a section mid-pipe (returns object unchanged)


ferx_set_section()
Rewrite a section mid-pipe (returns object unchanged)


ferx_model_edit()
Copy + open a model in your editor


ferx_model_validate()
Check that all required sections are present

title: Parameter transforms: log, logit, and additive ETAs
text:
ferx supports three parameterisation styles for individual parameters:
The optimiser always works on the unbounded or transformed scale; ferx back-transforms to the natural scale when reporting results.

title: Parameter transforms: log, logit, and additive ETAs
text:
Log-normal ETAs (default)
The standard warfarin model uses log-normal ETAs for CL, V, and KA:
ex <- ferx_example("warfarin")
ferx_model_inspect(ex$model)
After fitting, print() reports omega as [log-normal] with a CV%:
fit <- ferx_fit(ex$model, ex$data)
fit
# --- OMEGA Estimates ---
#   ETA_CL                   [log-normal]  = 0.042300  CV% = 20.8  SE = 0.012100
#   ETA_V                    [log-normal]  = 0.019800  CV% = 14.2  SE = 0.008900
#   ETA_KA                   [log-normal]  = 0.157000  CV% = 41.5  SE = 0.048000
The CV% is derived from the log-scale variance as:
\[\text{CV\%} = 100 \times \sqrt{\exp(\omega) - 1}\]
For small variances this is approximately \(100\sqrt{\omega}\), but the exact formula is always used.
ferx_estimates() returns omega rows with transform = "variance" (all omega rows use this label regardless of ETA parameterisation style — the log-normal interpretation is conveyed by print.ferx_fit(), not the tidy table):
ferx_estimates(fit)
#   param    transform  estimate      se  rse_pct  lower_95  upper_95
#   ETA_CL   variance     0.0423  0.0121     28.6    0.0186    0.0660
#   ETA_V    variance     0.0198  0.0089     44.9    0.0024    0.0372
#   ETA_KA   variance     0.1570  0.0480     30.6    0.0629    0.2511

title: Parameter transforms: log, logit, and additive ETAs
text:
Logit-normal ETA (bioavailability)
When a parameter is a fraction bounded between 0 and 1, use inv_logit / logit to ensure individual values stay in (0, 1):
F = inv_logit(logit(THETA_F) + ETA_F)
THETA_F is specified on the natural (0, 1) scale in [parameters]. When ETA_F = 0, F_i = THETA_F exactly (the logit and inv_logit cancel). The omega for ETA_F is the variance on the logit scale.
ex_logit <- ferx_example("warfarin_logit_f")
ferx_model_inspect(ex_logit$model)

fit_logit <- ferx_fit(ex_logit$model, ex_logit$data)
fit_logit
ferx_estimates() populates estimate_natural, lower_95_natural, and upper_95_natural for logit-transformed thetas. The CI is computed symmetrically on the logit scale and back-transformed through inv_logit, giving an asymmetric interval on the (0, 1) scale:
est <- ferx_estimates(fit_logit)
est[est$param == "THETA_F", ]
#   param    transform  estimate    se  rse_pct  estimate_natural  lower_95_natural  upper_95_natural
#   THETA_F  logit        0.847  0.143     16.9             0.700             0.638             0.755
The omega for ETA_F has transform = "variance" (same label as all omega rows):
est[est$param == "ETA_F", ]
#   param  transform  estimate     se  rse_pct
#   ETA_F  variance     0.0631  0.028     44.4

title: Parameter transforms: log, logit, and additive ETAs
text:
Additive ETA (lag time)
When the BSV on a parameter is additive — for example, a lag time where the subject-level deviation is in hours — use a plain sum:
TLAG = TVTLAG + ETA_TLAG
ETA_TLAG follows N(0, ω_TLAG), so individual lag times are normally distributed around TVTLAG.
ex_add <- ferx_example("warfarin_additive_eta")
ferx_model_inspect(ex_add$model)

fit_add <- ferx_fit(ex_add$model, ex_add$data)
fit_add
# --- OMEGA Estimates ---
#   ETA_TLAG                 [additive]    = 0.312000  SD = 0.5600  SE = N/A
ferx_estimates() returns transform = "variance" for the omega element (all omega rows). The estimate is the variance in the original units (hours²); no back-transform is applied:
ferx_estimates(fit_add)
#   param    transform  estimate     se  rse_pct
#   ETA_TLAG variance     0.312   0.098     31.4
# SD in original units = sqrt(0.312) ≈ 0.56 h

title: Parameter transforms: log, logit, and additive ETAs
text:
Mixing transforms in one model
All three styles can appear in the same model:
[individual_parameters]
  CL   = TVCL  * exp(ETA_CL)                    # log-normal
  V1   = TVV1  * exp(ETA_V1)                    # log-normal
  KA   = TVKA  * exp(ETA_KA)                    # log-normal
  F    = inv_logit(logit(THETA_F) + ETA_F)      # logit-normal
  TLAG = TVTLAG + ETA_TLAG                      # additive
Each ETA is handled independently; the omega matrix contains the corresponding variances on their respective scales.

title: Parameter transforms: log, logit, and additive ETAs
text:
Summary table









Transform
Omega interpretation
print() label
ferx_estimates()




Log-normal
Variance on log scale
[log-normal] + CV%
transform = "variance", symmetric CI on log-var scale


Logit-normal
Variance on logit scale
[logit] + SD_logit + ±1 SD interval
transform = "variance"; logit theta gets estimate_natural etc.


Additive
Variance in original units
[additive] + SD
transform = "variance", symmetric CI in original units²

title: ferx
text:
open-source NLME · v0.1.2


Fe·Rx




ferx fits models defined in a small, readable DSL — bracket-sectioned .ferx files — powered by a Rust backend with Enzyme automatic differentiation for exact gradients. FOCE/FOCEI estimation. MIT-licensed.


26 — iron, the element   2026 — launch year   MIT — license



R devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")


sh docker build -t ferx:latest .










[warfarin_pk.ferx] model

# one-compartment oral PK — warfarin

[parameters]
  theta TVCL(0.2, 0.001, 10.0)
  theta TVV(10.0, 0.1, 500.0)
  theta TVKA(1.5, 0.01, 50.0)
  omega ETA_CL ~ 0.09
  omega ETA_V  ~ 0.04
  omega ETA_KA ~ 0.30
  sigma PROP_ERR ~ 0.02

[individual_parameters]
  CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL)
  V  = TVV  * exp(ETA_V)
  KA = TVKA * exp(ETA_KA)

[structural_model]
  pk one_cpt_oral(cl=CL, v=V, ka=KA)

[error_model]
  DV ~ proportional(PROP_ERR)

title: ferx
text:
Learn ferx


Book
Comprehensive narrative guide — installation through ODE models, BLOQ, and IOV. All code runs on CI.


Get started
Install ferx and run the warfarin quickstart in under five minutes.


Reference
Function-level documentation for every exported function in ferx-r.


Model DSL
Complete syntax reference for .ferx model files.

title: Post-fit diagnostics
text: After fitting, ferx returns a rich ferx_fit object with all the diagnostics needed for model evaluation. This article covers the key diagnostic functions and fields.

title: Post-fit diagnostics
text:
Parameter estimates — ferx_estimates()
ferx_estimates() collects all estimated parameters (theta, omega, sigma) into a tidy data frame. Run it after any fit that used covariance = TRUE:
ex  <- ferx_example("warfarin")
fit <- ferx_fit(ex$model, ex$data)

ferx_estimates(fit)
#   param           transform    estimate      se  rse_pct  lower_95  upper_95
#   TVCL            identity       0.134  0.0108     8.1     0.113     0.155
#   TVV             identity       7.94   0.420      5.3     7.12      8.76
#   TVKA            identity       1.07   0.160     14.9     0.756     1.38
#   ETA_CL          variance       0.042  0.012     28.6    0.019     0.066
#   ETA_V           variance       0.020  0.009     44.9    0.002     0.037
#   ETA_KA          variance       0.157  0.048     30.6    0.063     0.251
#   PROP_ERR        proportional   0.011  0.001      9.1    0.009     0.013
Columns:







Column
Description




param
Parameter name


transform
Scale used by the estimator ("identity", "log", "logit", "variance" for omega)


estimate
Point estimate on the estimation scale


se
Standard error (requires covariance = TRUE)


rse_pct
Relative SE: |SE / estimate| × 100


lower_95, upper_95
95% CI on the estimation scale


estimate_natural
Back-transformed estimate (logit theta only)


lower_95_natural, upper_95_natural
Back-transformed CI (logit theta only)



See Parameter transforms for how the transform column relates to log-normal, logit-normal, and additive ETAs.

title: Post-fit diagnostics
text:
Parameter correlation matrix — ferx_cor_matrix()
ferx_cor_matrix() converts the estimated parameter covariance matrix to correlations. A value near ±1 between two parameters signals a structural identifiability problem worth investigating:
ferx_cor_matrix(fit)
#                TVCL   TVV  TVKA  ...
#   TVCL         1.00  0.12  0.03
#   TVV          0.12  1.00 -0.07
#   TVKA         0.03 -0.07  1.00
High correlations between CL and V often arise in poorly-sampled datasets where absorption and elimination are not separately identifiable.

title: Post-fit diagnostics
text:
ETA-covariate correlations — ferx_eta_cov()
ferx_eta_cov() computes Pearson correlations between individual ETA estimates (EBEs) and numeric covariate columns. Identifies covariates worth including in a formal covariate search.
The warfarin dataset has no covariate columns. The following example uses two_cpt_oral_cov, which includes body weight (WT) and creatinine clearance (CRCL):
ex_cov   <- ferx_example("two_cpt_oral_cov")
fit_cov  <- ferx_fit(ex_cov$model, ex_cov$data)
data_cov <- read.csv(ex_cov$data)
ferx_eta_cov(fit_cov, data_cov)
#   eta      covariate    r   p_val  flag
#   ETA_CL   WT        0.38  0.008   [!]
#   ETA_CL   CRCL      0.41  0.003   [!]
#   ETA_V1   WT        0.29  0.047
[!] flags correlations with |r| ≥ 0.3 as candidates for formal covariate testing. Pass the data frame (not the path); any numeric column that is constant within a subject and not a standard NONMEM column (TIME, DV, AMT, EVID, MDV, CMT, RATE) is treated as a covariate.

title: Post-fit diagnostics
text:
IWRES autocorrelation — check_diagnostics()
check_diagnostics() returns a structured list with an $autocorrelation data frame (Durbin-Watson + lag-1 Pearson r) and a tidy $shrinkage data frame covering all ETA and EPS components:
diag <- check_diagnostics(fit)

diag$autocorrelation
#   dw_statistic  lag1_r               flag
#           1.84  0.0821  no autocorrelation

diag$shrinkage
#    param type shrinkage shrinkage_pct
#   ETA_CL  eta     0.142          14.2
#    ETA_V  eta     0.283          28.3
#   ETA_KA  eta     0.452          45.2
#      EPS  eps     0.094           9.4
print(fit) shows the same values in a --- Diagnostics --- block. A message() with ordered remedies is emitted automatically when DW < 1.5 (positive autocorrelation) or DW > 2.5 (negative autocorrelation).








DW range
Interpretation
Suggested action




< 1.5
Positive autocorrelation
Check time ordering; add transit compartment; consider IOV on ka/F; SDE


1.5 – 2.5
No concern
—


> 2.5
Negative autocorrelation
Over-parameterisation or misspecified error model



For a full walkthrough with plots see Chapter 5 (Diagnostics) in the ferx book.

title: Post-fit diagnostics
text:
Goodness-of-fit plots from fit$sdtab
fit$sdtab is a data frame with one row per observation containing PRED, IPRED, CWRES, and IWRES:
sdtab <- fit$sdtab
Standard GOF plots with ggplot2:
library(ggplot2)

# DV vs PRED
ggplot(sdtab, aes(PRED, DV)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "DV vs PRED", x = "Population prediction", y = "Observed")

# DV vs IPRED
ggplot(sdtab, aes(IPRED, DV)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "DV vs IPRED", x = "Individual prediction", y = "Observed")

# CWRES vs TIME
ggplot(sdtab, aes(TIME, CWRES)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 0, 2),
             linetype = c("dashed", "solid", "dashed"), colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "CWRES vs TIME")

# CWRES vs PRED
ggplot(sdtab, aes(PRED, CWRES)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 0, 2),
             linetype = c("dashed", "solid", "dashed"), colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "CWRES vs PRED")

title: Post-fit diagnostics
text:
Other ferx_fit fields
fit$ofv                  # objective function value (-2 log-likelihood)
fit$aic                  # AIC = OFV + 2p
fit$bic                  # BIC = OFV + p*ln(N)
fit$converged            # TRUE if the outer optimizer converged
fit$method               # estimation method used
fit$gradient_used        # "enzyme", "dual", or "finite_differences"
fit$warnings             # non-fatal warnings from the run

fit$ebe_etas             # empirical Bayes ETAs per subject (data frame)
fit$individual_estimates # individual PK parameters (CL, V, KA, ...) per subject
fit$eta_normality        # Shapiro-Wilk normality test p-value per ETA

fit$shrinkage_eta        # ETA shrinkage per random effect
fit$shrinkage_eps        # EPS shrinkage (scalar or vector for multi-sigma models)
fit$dw_statistic         # pooled Durbin-Watson statistic for IWRES
fit$iwres_lag1_r         # pooled lag-1 Pearson r for IWRES
Shrinkage — values near 1 indicate that the data are not informative about a random effect:
\[\text{ETA shrinkage}_k = 1 - \frac{\text{SD}(\hat{\eta}_k)}{\sqrt{\omega_{kk}}} \qquad \text{EPS shrinkage} = 1 - \text{SD}(\text{IWRES})\]

title: Post-fit diagnostics
text:
Convergence diagnostics — ferx_check_init()
Before committing to a long fit, run a 5-iteration pilot to confirm the OFV is decreasing from the initial values:
chk <- ferx_check_init(ex$model, ex$data, method = "focei")
chk$summary
#   n_iter  ofv_start  ofv_end  ofv_drop  converged
#        5     -180.2   -186.3      6.1      FALSE

title: Post-fit diagnostics
text:
Optimizer trace — ferx_trace() / ferx_plot_trace()
When optimizer_trace = TRUE is passed to ferx_fit(), ferx writes a per-iteration CSV. Read and plot it:
fit <- ferx_fit(ex$model, ex$data, optimizer_trace = TRUE)

tr <- ferx_trace(fit)          # data frame with one row per outer iteration
head(tr[, c("iter", "ofv", "grad_norm")])

ferx_plot_trace(fit)           # two-panel plot: OFV + method-specific metric
ferx_plot_trace(fit, log_ofv = TRUE)  # OFV on log scale (OFV - min(OFV))
ferx_trace() with no argument recalls the trace from the last ferx_fit() call in the current session:
ferx_trace()    # same as ferx_trace(fit) after the above
See Output Reference — Optimizer trace CSV for the full column reference.

title: Simulation and prediction
text: ferx provides two post-fit prediction functions and a built-in simulation mode for model validation.

title: Simulation and prediction
text:
ferx_simulate() — stochastic simulation
ferx_simulate() draws random effects from the estimated omega and generates simulated observations, including residual error:
ex  <- ferx_example("warfarin")
fit <- ferx_fit(ex$model, ex$data)   # fit first

# 100 simulated replicates
sim <- ferx_simulate(ex$model, ex$data,
                     n_sim = 100,
                     seed  = 42,
                     fit   = fit)
head(sim)
#   SIM  ID  TIME  IPRED  DV_SIM
#     1   1   0.5  9.81   10.2
#     1   1   1.0  14.3   13.6
#   ...
Key arguments:







Argument
Description




model
Path to a .ferx model file, or a ferx_model object


data
Path to a NONMEM CSV, or a ferx_model that carries a data path


n_sim
Number of simulation replicates (default: 1)


seed
RNG seed for reproducibility


fit
A ferx_fit object to use fitted parameters; if NULL, uses the initial values from the model file



Output columns: SIM, ID, TIME, IPRED (individual prediction without residual error), DV_SIM (simulated observation including residual error).

Visual predictive check (VPC)
A VPC overlays observed data with simulation percentiles to assess model fit:
library(ggplot2)
obs  <- read.csv(ex$data)
obs  <- obs[obs$EVID == 0, ]   # observations only

# Compute 5th / 50th / 95th percentiles of simulated DV at each time
vpc <- aggregate(DV_SIM ~ TIME, sim, quantile, probs = c(0.05, 0.5, 0.95))

ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(data = vpc,
              aes(x = TIME, ymin = DV_SIM[, "5%"], ymax = DV_SIM[, "95%"]),
              fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_line(data = vpc, aes(x = TIME, y = DV_SIM[, "50%"]),
            colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(data = obs, aes(x = TIME, y = DV), alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Visual Predictive Check",
       x = "Time (h)", y = "Concentration")

title: Simulation and prediction
text:
ferx_predict() — population predictions
ferx_predict() computes population predictions using fitted theta values with all ETAs set to zero (i.e. the typical individual):
pred <- ferx_predict(ex$model, ex$data, fit = fit)
head(pred)
#   ID  TIME  PRED
#    1   0.5  10.12
#    1   1.0  14.87
#   ...
This is equivalent to fit$sdtab$PRED but can be used without a full fit object (e.g. to evaluate predictions from externally specified parameters).

title: Simulation and prediction
text:
[simulation] block — built-in virtual trial
For standalone simulations without real data, add a [simulation] block to the .ferx file:
[simulation]
  subjects = 100
  dose     = 100
  cmt      = 1
  seed     = 12345
  times    = [0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24]



Key
Description




subjects
Number of virtual subjects


dose
Dose amount administered to each subject


cmt
Dosing compartment (1-indexed)


seed
Random seed for reproducibility


times
Observation time points



Run from the CLI:
ferx model.ferx --simulate
This will:

Parse the model and [simulation] block
Sample random effects: \(\eta_i \sim N(0, \Omega)\)
Compute individual parameters via [individual_parameters]
Generate predictions with the structural model
Add residual error: \(DV = IPRED + \varepsilon\), \(\varepsilon \sim N(0, V)\)
Report parameter estimates and diagnostics

title: Simulation and prediction
text:
Simulation process (R and CLI)
For each simulated subject:

Sample \(\eta_i \sim N(0, \hat{\Omega})\) (from fitted or specified omega)
Compute individual PK parameters via the [individual_parameters] expressions
Generate IPRED with the structural model at the dose and observation times
Sample residual error: \(\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, V_{ij})\) where \(V_{ij}\) is evaluated at IPRED
\(DV_{ij} = IPRED_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}\)

title: Simulation and prediction
FAQ
text:
Do I need to write explicit MU-referencing like in NONMEM or nlmixr2?
No. You do not need to write an explicit MU_i intermediate variable. ferx detects the same structure automatically from the right-hand side of each [individual_parameters] line.
In NONMEM and nlmixr2, MU-referencing is required in SAEM because the conjugate-Gibbs E-step is only valid when the ETA→THETA relationship is strictly linear on a transformed scale. In ferx, you simply write the individual-parameter expression naturally:
CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL)              # detected: multiplicative / log-normal
CL = exp(log(TVCL) + ETA_CL)         # detected: canonical MU form
CL = TVCL * (WT/70)^0.75 * exp(ETA_CL)  # detected: with covariate
CL = TVCL + ETA_CL                   # detected: additive
The parser records the ETA→THETA mapping and re-centres the inner-loop ETA search at each outer iteration.
Patterns not auto-detected (falls back to eta = 0 start):

Two or more free thetas in the product (TVCL * TVSCALE * exp(ETA_CL)) — ambiguous anchor
Compound eta expressions inside exp (exp(ETA_CL + ETA_OCC))

Mu-referencing affects convergence speed and robustness, not the final MLE. Disable with mu_referencing = false in [fit_options] to reproduce pre-automatic behaviour.

title: FAQ
text:
Do I need to write explicit MU-referencing like in NONMEM or nlmixr2?
No. You do not need to write an explicit MU_i intermediate variable. ferx detects the same structure automatically from the right-hand side of each [individual_parameters] line.
In NONMEM and nlmixr2, MU-referencing is required in SAEM because the conjugate-Gibbs E-step is only valid when the ETA→THETA relationship is strictly linear on a transformed scale. In ferx, you simply write the individual-parameter expression naturally:
CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL)              # detected: multiplicative / log-normal
CL = exp(log(TVCL) + ETA_CL)         # detected: canonical MU form
CL = TVCL * (WT/70)^0.75 * exp(ETA_CL)  # detected: with covariate
CL = TVCL + ETA_CL                   # detected: additive
The parser records the ETA→THETA mapping and re-centres the inner-loop ETA search at each outer iteration.
Patterns not auto-detected (falls back to eta = 0 start):

Two or more free thetas in the product (TVCL * TVSCALE * exp(ETA_CL)) — ambiguous anchor
Compound eta expressions inside exp (exp(ETA_CL + ETA_OCC))

Mu-referencing affects convergence speed and robustness, not the final MLE. Disable with mu_referencing = false in [fit_options] to reproduce pre-automatic behaviour.

title: FAQ
FAQ
text:
Optimizer selection
Which outer optimizer should I pick?
slsqp (the default) is the right choice for most models — it is fast, handles box constraints cleanly, and behaves well on the log-transformed parameter scale.
Reach for a different optimizer when SLSQP misbehaves:

bobyqa — derivative-free, robust when FOCE’s FD gradients are noisy and SLSQP stalls or oscillates. Slower per iteration on smooth problems.
trust_region — second-order Newton trust-region with BHHH Hessian and AD gradient. Can be faster near convergence. The Steihaug CG budget is adaptive by default (ceil(sqrt(n_params))); override with steihaug_max_iters if needed.
lbfgs / bfgs — fall back to these only when NLopt is unavailable.

See Fit Options — Optimizer choices.

title: FAQ
text:
Block omega
How do I switch from diagonal to block omega?
Each omega variance becomes a diagonal entry; off-diagonals start at 0.0:
# Before
omega ETA_CL ~ 0.1
omega ETA_V1 ~ 0.1
omega ETA_Q  ~ 0.1

# After (3×3 block, 6 values: 1+2+3)
block_omega (ETA_CL, ETA_V1, ETA_Q) = [
  0.1,
  0.0, 0.1,
  0.0, 0.0, 0.1
]
Block omega regularises poorly-identified parameters by letting them share variability. Starting all off-diagonals at 0.0 lets the fit recover the correlations from the data. See Parameters — Block omega.

title: FAQ
text:
Sigma scale
Is sigma estimated on the SD scale or the variance scale?
SD scale. sigma PROP_ERR ~ 0.1 for a proportional model is a 10% CV initial value.
The fit YAML emits both estimate (SD) and variance (SD²). For proportional components it also emits cv_pct = estimate * 100. See Error Model — Sigma scale.

title: FAQ
text:
FOCEI vs FOCE
When should I use FOCEI instead of FOCE?
Use FOCEI (default) whenever the error model is proportional or combined. FOCE evaluates the residual variance at η = 0 (population mean); FOCEI evaluates it at the individual EBE η̂, capturing the dependence between residual error and individual predictions.
For a purely additive error model, FOCE and FOCEI are equivalent (the interaction term is zero). Using FOCE for additive-error models can save a small amount of computation.

title: FAQ
text:
ODE vs analytical
When must I use an ODE model?
Use an ODE model when the kinetics cannot be expressed as a sum of exponentials:

Saturable (Michaelis-Menten) elimination
Target-mediated drug disposition (TMDD)
Transit compartment absorption
Custom multi-compartment structures

For standard first-order kinetics use the analytical models (one_cpt_oral, two_cpt_oral, etc.) — they are 10–100× faster and support Enzyme autodiff gradients. See ODE Models.

title: FAQ
text:
Porting from NONMEM
How do I port a NONMEM model to ferx?

Drop all $ABBR, $MU_i, $SIZE boilerplate — not needed in ferx.
Translate $PK assignments to [individual_parameters] expressions with = instead of =.
Replace THETA(i), ETA(i), EPS(i) with descriptive names (TVCL, ETA_CL, PROP_ERR).
If the NONMEM model uses explicit MU_i = LOG(THETA(i)) lines, drop them — ferx detects the mu-reference automatically.
Map $SUBROUTINE ADVAN to the equivalent ferx pk function:

ADVAN1 → one_cpt_iv_bolus
ADVAN2 → one_cpt_oral
ADVAN3 → two_cpt_iv_bolus
ADVAN4 → two_cpt_oral
ADVAN11 → three_cpt_iv_bolus
ADVAN12 → three_cpt_oral

Map $ERROR to [error_model]. Remember sigma is on the SD scale in ferx (not variance).

title: FAQ
text:
Gradient methods
When does Enzyme autodiff kick in, and when does ferx fall back to finite differences?
The gradient path is resolved per-subject at runtime:







Path
When used




AdSingleSnapshot (Enzyme reverse-mode)
Subject has no time-varying covariates; analytical PK model


AdEventDriven (Enzyme reverse-mode, event-driven)
Subject has TV covariates; 1- or 2-cpt analytical model


Fd (central finite differences)
FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1 build, ODE model, or TV-cov on 3-cpt model



fit$gradient_used (in R) shows which path was resolved. See Autodiff for details.

title: ODE Solver
text: ferx-core includes an adaptive Runge-Kutta solver for models whose structural equations cannot be expressed analytically. It is invoked whenever a .ferx model file contains an [odes] block; analytical PK models bypass it entirely.

title: ODE Solver
text:
Algorithm: Dormand-Prince RK45
The solver implements the Dormand-Prince method (also called RK45 or DOPRI5) — the same family as Julia’s Tsit5(). It is an explicit, embedded Runge-Kutta pair:

5th-order solution used for advancing the state
4th-order embedded solution used only for error estimation
7 function evaluations per step (the last stage is reused as the first of the next step — FSAL property)

The Butcher tableau coefficients are hardcoded in ode/solver.rs from the original Dormand & Prince (1980) paper.
Default tolerances:



Parameter
Default




abstol
1e-6


reltol
1e-4


max_steps
10 000


initial_dt
0.1


min_dt
1e-12



These are appropriate for typical PK ODE systems (2–5 states, smooth dynamics, concentration units in μg/mL or mg/L). Tighter tolerances can be set via [fit_options] if needed.

title: ODE Solver
text:
Adaptive step control
At each candidate step the solver computes the scaled error norm:
err = ‖(y₅ − y₄) / (abstol + reltol · |y₅|)‖₂ / √n

If err ≤ 1: step accepted; next step size scaled up by min(5, 0.9 · err^(-0.2))
If err > 1: step rejected and retried at max(0.2, 0.9 · err^(-0.25)) · h

The solver errors if h falls below min_dt (signals a stiff or ill-posed system) or if the step count exceeds max_steps.

title: ODE Solver
ODE Solver
text:
Defining an ODE model
The [odes] section of a .ferx file specifies the right-hand side using a simple expression DSL. Derivatives are written as d/dt(STATE):
[odes]
  d/dt(depot)   = -KA * depot
  d/dt(central) = KA * depot - (CL / V) * central
Individual parameters from [individual_parameters] (CL, V, KA, …) are available as symbols. The observable compartment is matched to the CMT column in the data.

title: ODE Solver
section: When to use ODE vs analytical
text:
When to use ODE vs analytical







Situation
Recommendation




1-cpt or 2-cpt oral/IV, standard dosing
Use analytical (pk one_cpt_oral(...))


PD link model, transit compartments, enterohepatic circulation
Use [odes]


Saturable elimination (Michaelis-Menten), parallel pathways
Use [odes]



Analytical models are typically 10–50× faster per likelihood evaluation and support the Enzyme AD gradient path. ODE models currently fall back to finite-difference gradients.

title: ODE Solver
text:
Relevant source files







File
Role




ode/solver.rs
solve_ode(), Dormand-Prince coefficients, adaptive step loop


ode/predictions.rs
ode_predictions(), event handling, break-time construction


ode/mod.rs
Module re-exports

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text: ferx-core supports five estimation methods: FOCE, FOCEI, SAEM, Gauss-Newton, and a GN+FOCEI hybrid. FOCEI is the default. All methods share the same two-loop structure: an outer loop over population parameters and an inner loop over per-subject empirical Bayes estimates (EBEs).

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
FOCE vs FOCEI
FOCE (First-Order Conditional Estimation) linearises the model around the EBE η̂ and evaluates the residual variance R at the population mean (η = 0):
\[\text{OFV}_i = (y - f_0)^T \tilde{R}^{-1} (y - f_0) + \log|\tilde{R}|\]
where \(f_0 = f(\hat{\eta}) - H\hat{\eta}\) and \(\tilde{R} = H\Omega H^T + R(f_0)\).
FOCEI adds the interaction term — the residual variance \(V(\hat{\eta})\) is evaluated at η̂ rather than η = 0, capturing the dependence of residual error on individual predictions:
\[\text{OFV}_i = (y - \hat{f})^T V^{-1} (y - \hat{f}) + \hat{\eta}^T \Omega^{-1} \hat{\eta} + \log|\tilde{R}|\]
FOCEI is more accurate when the error model is proportional or combined. Use FOCE only for additive-error models where the interaction term is zero.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
The two-loop structure

Outer loop — population parameters
Minimises the population OFV = Σᵢ OFVᵢ over θ, Ω, σ. Parameters are internally log-transformed (theta, sigma) and Cholesky-factored (omega) so the optimizer works on an unconstrained packed vector:
x = [log(θ_free), chol(Ω_BSV), log(σ), chol(Ω_IOV)]
Available optimizers (set via optimizer in [fit_options]):








Key
Algorithm
Notes




slsqp
NLopt SLSQP
Default. Gradient-based, handles bounds well


bobyqa
NLopt BOBYQA
Derivative-free; robust when FD gradients are noisy


nlopt_lbfgs
NLopt L-BFGS
Good for large parameter spaces


mma
NLopt MMA
Method of Moving Asymptotes


bfgs
Built-in BFGS
Quasi-Newton with backtracking line search


lbfgs
Built-in L-BFGS
Memory-efficient BFGS


trust_region
Newton trust-region (argmin)
AD gradient + BHHH Hessian; Steihaug CG subproblem



Global search: setting global_search = true runs NLopt CRS2-LM (Controlled Random Search) as a gradient-free pre-search before the local optimizer. Helps escape local minima on poorly-identified models. Budget is controlled by global_maxeval (default: 200 * (n_params + 1)).


Inner loop — empirical Bayes estimates
Minimises the individual NLL per subject independently (parallelised via rayon):
\[\text{NLL}(\eta) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\eta^T\Omega^{-1}\eta + \log|\Omega| + \sum_j\left(\frac{(y_j - f_j(\hat{\eta}))^2}{V_j(\hat{\eta})} + \log V_j(\hat{\eta})\right)\right]\]
Uses L-BFGS with automatic differentiation gradients (preferred) or finite differences. Falls back to Nelder-Mead simplex if L-BFGS fails to converge.
Warm starting: EBEs from the previous outer iteration seed the inner optimizer, drastically reducing inner iterations in later stages when parameters change slowly.
EBE convergence guard: if more than max_unconverged_frac (default 0.1) of subjects have unconverged EBEs, the outer optimizer receives OFV = ∞ for that step, preventing convergence to a solution with unreliable EBEs.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Convergence criteria



Loop
Criterion
Default




Outer
gradient norm < outer_gtol
1e-6


Outer
max iterations
maxiter = 500


Inner
gradient norm < inner_tol
1e-4


Inner
max iterations
inner_maxiter = 200



inner_tol = 1e-4 matches the precision of NONMEM’s default inner-loop convergence (SIGDIGITS ≈ 3). Tighter values over-converge EBEs relative to the Sheiner-Beal linearisation error and can slow FOCEI fits 10–15× without changing the final OFV.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
OFV / AIC / BIC
\[\text{OFV} = -2\log L \qquad \text{AIC} = \text{OFV} + 2p \qquad \text{BIC} = \text{OFV} + p\log N\]
where \(p\) is the number of estimated parameters and \(N\) is the number of observations.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Covariance step
When covariance = true, ferx computes the observed Fisher information matrix via finite-difference Hessian of the population OFV at convergence:

Standard errors = \(\sqrt{\text{diag}(\hat{\Sigma})}\)
%RSE = \(|\text{SE} / \hat{\theta}| \times 100\)
Omega SEs derived via delta method from the Cholesky parameterisation

If the Hessian is not positive definite or cannot be inverted, ferx emits a warning ("Covariance step failed") and sets covariance_status = Failed; SE fields are None. A future release will include eigenvalue diagnostics in the warning to help distinguish near-zero eigenvalues from identifiability problems (ferx-core#17).

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
SAEM
Stochastic Approximation EM replaces the deterministic inner loop with Metropolis-Hastings sampling, following the Monolix two-phase schedule:

Phase 1 (exploration, γ = 1): rapid basin finding; default 150 iterations
Phase 2 (convergence, γ = 1/k): decreasing-weight average; default 250 iterations

Each SAEM iteration:

E-step: sample η per subject via MH random walk (\(\eta_\text{prop} = \eta + \delta_i \cdot L \cdot z\), \(z \sim N(0,I)\))
SA update: \(S_2 \leftarrow (1-\gamma_k)S_2 + \gamma_k \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \eta_i\eta_i^T\)
M-step for Ω: \(\Omega_k = S_2\) (closed form, with structural zeros projected out)
M-step for θ and σ: NLopt minimises the conditional observation NLL with ETAs fixed. When mu_referencing = true (default), log-normal thetas use the closed-form EM update instead: \(\log\theta_j \leftarrow \log\theta_j + \gamma_k\overline{\eta_j}\)
Adaptation: per-subject MH step sizes \(\delta_i\) adjusted every adapt_interval iterations to target ~40% acceptance rate

Post-SAEM: standard FOCE inner loop refines EBEs from the SAEM ETAs, then FOCE OFV, AIC, BIC, and (optionally) the covariance matrix are computed so results are directly comparable with FOCE fits.
[fit_options]
  method        = saem
  n_exploration = 150
  n_convergence = 250
  n_mh_steps    = 3
  seed          = 12345
  covariance    = true
SAEM + FOCEI polish (recommended for complex models):
[fit_options]
  method     = [saem, focei]
  covariance = true
Limitation: SAEM is not supported with IOV models.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Gauss-Newton
The Gauss-Newton optimizer exploits the nonlinear-least-squares structure of the FOCE objective using the BHHH Hessian approximation:
\[H_\text{BHHH} = 4 \sum_i g_i g_i^T\]
where \(g_i = \nabla_x \text{NLL}_i\) is the per-subject gradient. Levenberg-Marquardt damping \(H_\text{LM} = H_\text{BHHH} + \lambda\,\text{diag}(H_\text{BHHH})\) stabilises steps away from the minimum; \(\lambda\) adapts: ×0.3 on success, ×10 on rejection.








Method
Key
Description




Pure GN
gn
Typically converges in 10–30 iterations


GN + FOCEI hybrid
gn_hybrid
GN finds the basin; FOCEI polishes (up to 100 iter via SLSQP)



Use gn for fast feedback during model development; gn_hybrid when you want robustness as well as speed.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Inter-Occasion Variability (IOV)
IOV models occasion-to-occasion shifts in PK parameters within a subject. Kappa (κ) is the IOV random effect, re-drawn independently per occasion:
\[\kappa_{ik} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Omega_\text{IOV})\]
Option A — diagonal IOV (independent kappas):
[parameters]
  kappa KAPPA_CL ~ 0.05
  kappa KAPPA_V  ~ 0.03

[individual_parameters]
  CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL + KAPPA_CL)

[fit_options]
  iov_column = OCC
Option B — block IOV (correlated kappas):
block_kappa (KAPPA_CL, KAPPA_V) = [0.05, 0.01, 0.03]
Mixed Option A + B declarations are supported; a name may not appear in both.
The inner loop uses find_ebe_iov instead of find_ebe when kappas are present, jointly optimising BSV etas and all per-occasion kappas. The outer loop packs IOV omega after sigma in the packed vector. AD is not used for IOV gradients — finite differences are used instead.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Sampling Importance Resampling (SIR)
SIR is an optional post-estimation step that provides non-parametric parameter uncertainty estimates, more robust than the asymptotic covariance matrix for small datasets or boundary estimates.

Draw M parameter vectors from a multivariate Student-t proposal centred on \(\hat{\theta}\) with covariance \(\hat{\Sigma}\) and degrees of freedom ν (default ν = 5). The heavy tails relative to Normal help cover the posterior when the proposal covariance underestimates uncertainty.
Compute importance weights from OFV differences
Resample m vectors proportional to weights

The effective sample size (ESS) diagnostic indicates fit quality: ESS > m is excellent; ESS < 100 suggests the proposal is a poor match.
[fit_options]
  method           = focei
  covariance       = true
  sir              = true
  sir_samples      = 1000
  sir_resamples    = 250
  sir_seed         = 42
  sir_df           = 5       # Student-t degrees of freedom (default 5)
  sir_keep_samples = false   # set true to access raw proposal draws
Reference: Dosne et al. (2017), J Pharmacokinet Pharmacodyn 44(6):539-562.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Mu-referencing
When mu_referencing = true (default), ferx detects log-normal parameters from [individual_parameters] and re-centres ETA estimates on the current population mean each inner iteration. This improves conditioning for the SAEM M-step (closed-form EM update) and speeds inner-loop convergence for gradient-based methods. Disable with mu_referencing = false to reproduce pre-automatic-mu behaviour.

title: Overview
text:
ferx-core is the Rust computation engine that powers the ferx R package. It implements the full nonlinear mixed effects (NLME) modeling pipeline: model parsing, data ingestion, population parameter estimation (FOCE/FOCEI, SAEM, Gauss-Newton), simulation, and prediction. The R package (ferx-r) is a thin wrapper that exposes this engine via an extendr FFI boundary.
The crate is named ferx-nlme and lives at github.com/FeRx-NLME/ferx-core. ferx-r references it as a path dependency in src/rust/Cargo.toml.

title: Overview
text:
Stack position
R API  (ferx-r:  R/*.R)
  └─► extendr FFI  (ferx-r:  src/rust/src/lib.rs)
        └─► ferx-core  (estimation, PK, ODE, autodiff)
The FFI layer exposes three #[extendr] functions — ferx_rust_fit, ferx_rust_simulate, ferx_rust_predict — and converts ferx-core result types into R lists and data frames via fit_result_to_list() and sdtab_to_dataframe(). All heavy computation stays inside ferx-core.

title: Output Reference
text: A ferx-core run produces diagnostic data and parameter estimates that are exposed to R through the FitResult struct. The R layer formats these as data frames, named lists, and S3 objects. The CLI writes them as CSV and YAML files.

title: Output Reference
text:
sdtab — per-observation diagnostics
FitResult::sdtab is a Vec<SdtabRow> exposed to R as a data frame via sdtab_to_dataframe(). The CLI writes it as {model}-sdtab.csv.

Columns



Column
Description




ID
Subject identifier


TIME
Observation time


DV
Observed value


PRED
Population prediction (ETA = 0)


IPRED
Individual prediction (ETA = EBE)


CWRES
Conditional weighted residual


IWRES
Individual weighted residual


ETA1, ETA2, …
Empirical Bayes estimates of each random effect


EBE_OFV
Subject’s contribution to the total OFV


N_OBS
Number of observations for the subject





Residual definitions
IWRES (Individual Weighted Residual):
\[\text{IWRES}_j = \frac{y_j - \text{IPRED}_j}{\sqrt{V_j}}\]
where \(V_j\) is the residual variance evaluated at the individual prediction (\(\hat{\eta}\)). Measures how well the individual model fits each observation.
CWRES (Conditional Weighted Residual):
\[\text{CWRES}_j = \frac{y_j - f_{0,j}}{\sqrt{\tilde{R}_{jj}}}\]
where \(f_0 = f(\hat{\eta}) - H\hat{\eta}\) is the linearised population prediction and \(\tilde{R} = H\Omega H^T + R\) is the conditional variance matrix. CWRES accounts for the linearisation; it is the residual most appropriate for assessing population model fit.

title: Output Reference
text:
Shrinkage
Reported in FitResult after every fit. High shrinkage (near 1) indicates that the data are not informative about a random effect — individual ETAs collapse toward zero.
ETA shrinkage (per random effect, shrinkage_eta: Vec<f64>):
\[\text{shrinkage}_k = 1 - \frac{\text{SD}(\hat{\eta}_k)}{\sqrt{\omega_{kk}}}\]
EPS shrinkage (scalar, shrinkage_eps: f64):
\[\text{shrinkage}_\varepsilon = 1 - \text{SD}(\text{IWRES})\]
Values of NaN indicate a zero-variance omega component (ETA) or fewer than two valid residuals (EPS).

title: Output Reference
text:
Fit YAML ({model}-fit.yaml)
The CLI writes a YAML summary alongside the sdtab. Structure:
model:
  converged: true
  method: focei
objective_function:
  ofv: -280.1838
  aic: -266.1838
  bic: -247.2804
data:
  n_subjects: 10
  n_observations: 110
  n_parameters: 7
theta:
  TVCL:
    estimate: 0.132735
    se: 0.014549
    rse_pct: 11.0
  TVV:
    estimate: 7.694842
    se: 0.293028
    rse_pct: 3.8
omega:
  omega_11:
    variance: 0.028584
    cv_pct: 16.9
    se: 0.006394
  omega_22:
    variance: 0.009613
    cv_pct: 9.8
    se: 0.002165
sigma:
  sigma_1:
    estimate: 0.010638
    se: 0.000788
Key fields:

ofv — −2 log-likelihood
aic / bic — OFV + 2p / OFV + p·ln(N); p = estimated parameters, N = observations
rse_pct — relative SE as percentage: |SE / estimate| × 100
cv_pct (omega) — coefficient of variation: \(100\sqrt{\omega_{kk}}\)
se — standard error from the finite-difference covariance step

title: Fit Options
text:
Estimation methods

FOCE
method = foce
First-Order Conditional Estimation. Linearises the model around the EBE η̂ and evaluates residual variance at η = 0. Fast and reliable for additive-error models.


FOCEI (default)
method = focei
FOCE with Interaction. Evaluates residual variance at η̂ rather than η = 0, capturing the dependence of residual error on individual predictions. More accurate than FOCE when the error model is proportional or combined.


SAEM
method = saem
Stochastic Approximation EM. Uses Metropolis-Hastings sampling instead of MAP optimisation for random effects. More robust to local minima; recommended for complex models with many random effects. Not supported with IOV.


Gauss-Newton
method = gn
method = gn_hybrid
Gauss-Newton with BHHH Hessian approximation and Levenberg-Marquardt damping. gn typically converges in 10–30 iterations; gn_hybrid uses GN to find the basin and then polishes with FOCEI. Use gn for fast feedback during model development.


Chained methods
Pass a list to run stages sequentially, each starting from the previous converged parameters:
method = [saem, focei]
A SAEM→FOCEI chain is recommended for complex models: SAEM finds the basin robustly, FOCEI polishes to the MLE and produces a valid covariance matrix.

title: Fit Options
text:
Optimizer choices








Optimizer
Algorithm
Recommended for




slsqp
NLopt SLSQP (default)
General use


bfgs
Built-in BFGS quasi-Newton
When NLopt is unavailable


lbfgs
Built-in L-BFGS
Large parameter spaces


nlopt_lbfgs
NLopt L-BFGS
Alternative L-BFGS


mma
NLopt Method of Moving Asymptotes
Constrained problems


bobyqa
NLopt BOBYQA — derivative-free
Noisy/non-smooth objectives where FD gradients are unreliable


trust_region
Newton trust-region with Steihaug CG
Well-conditioned problems with small parameter count



bobyqa does not use gradients — robust to small discontinuities in the FOCE surface caused by EBE re-estimation, but converges more slowly on smooth problems.
trust_region uses an AD-based gradient and a BHHH approximate Hessian (H ≈ 4 Σ gᵢgᵢᵀ), which is always positive semi-definite and costs no extra OFV evaluations beyond the gradient. The Steihaug CG budget defaults to ceil(sqrt(n_params)) (typically 5 for standard NLME models); override with steihaug_max_iters if needed.

title: Fit Options
text:
SAEM-specific options








Key
Default
Description




n_exploration
150
Phase 1 iterations (step size γ = 1)


n_convergence
250
Phase 2 iterations (step size γ = 1/k)


n_mh_steps
3
Metropolis-Hastings steps per subject per iteration

50
Iterations between MH step-size adaptation


seed
12345
RNG seed for reproducibility

title: Fit Options
text:
Global search
global_search = true
Runs NLopt CRS2-LM (Controlled Random Search with Local Mutation) as a gradient-free pre-search before the local optimizer. Samples within parameter bounds; the local optimizer starts from the best point found. Useful for poorly-identified models or when the local optimizer lands in degenerate basins.








Key
Default
Description




global_search
false
Enable CRS2-LM pre-search


global_maxeval
200 * (n_params + 1)
Max OFV evaluations during global search



Requires a full NLopt build — a warning is emitted if CRS2-LM is unavailable.

title: Fit Options
text:
SIR (Sampling Importance Resampling)
SIR provides non-parametric parameter uncertainty estimates as an optional post-estimation step. Requires covariance = true.



Key
Default
Description




sir
false
Enable SIR


sir_samples
1000
Number of proposal samples (M)


sir_resamples
250
Number of resampled vectors (m)


sir_seed
